## [00:00 - 03:02]

In this lesson, you will learn all the questions that you need to ask when you are signing up rates from the carrier. When you're working with the carrier, you want to understand all the different costs associated to one delivery because if you don't understand all the costs, you're going to end up paying a lot more than what you anticipated. So it's super important to ask all the questions from very, very early on and see if you can negotiate every component of it. What I normally ask is the first one is what is your cost structure? And 90% of the carriers basically quote you two components. The first bit is a basic charge. They call it a consignment charge. The second bit is weight charge. It's based on the weight and usually like say from taking Melbourne to Sydney as an example. So it's usually about 20 to 30 cents per kilogram and just on the weight after I have to take that out and talk about it because the weight here is not always the same weight as what we interpreted. Basically from in the logistic world, they calculate the volume weight and if the volume weight is larger than the dead weight, you actually have to use the volume weight. The dead weight is usually just like for example, something that's 10 kilos, you might not get charged for 10 kilos, you get charged for 20 kilos because the volume weight is a lot larger. How do you calculate the volume weight is basically the length times width times height in centimeters divided by 1 million and times 250 and you will get the exact volume weight for the product. Most logistic company will charge you based on the larger weight when they do comparison between the volume weight and dead weight and a lot of time the volume weight is actually larger than the dead weight unless it's a heavy machinery. It's important to understand all the specs of your product. So basically you should have a product skew list of all your products and you should calculate all the volume weight for all your products and so you know how much the carrier will charge you based on so that there's no surprises and we'll do a comparison to the dead weight and in all the product business that I've done, most of the time the volume weight is larger than the dead weight and you should just use your volume weight as the base cost to calculate your delivery charge. There are a few other questions I normally ask.

## [03:02 - 06:05]

The first one is do you charge a re-delivery fee and most company would charge so it sometimes can be quite high and especially if you're selling low value item this can be very very very expensive. You want to be able to discover how much the re-delivery charge is and if you can negotiate that down. The second one is if ATL an option, what is ATL? ATL is basically authority to leave. If people's not home and can the delivery man leave the parcel at the door or with a concierge in the apartment, if ATL is available you can basically choose this service to avoid any re-delivery charge. And number three, if people are not collecting the parcel from the post office or from their local depot, do you actually charge a storage fee for the product that I'm sending? So those are a few questions that I normally ask the carrier. And on top of this there's another one. This is an interesting one and you probably don't see this a lot is that some carrier actually charge residential surcharge when they deliver to residential addresses. The example here is TNT here in Australia and FedEx in the US. They actually charge on average I think $5 for residential address. So with ATL, so this is an interesting one, it really depends on from business to business, depending on the value of the product, depends on your margin, etc. And you can definitely let customers choose themselves whether they would accept ATL and if they would be responsible if the item is lost or stolen. So you can definitely let the customer decide what they want to do. With re-delivery, it is what it is, you just have to factor in your margin. And with the residential surcharge and what I recommend you do is if you're delivering to residential address, you don't use the company that's charged your surcharge on top of the delivery charge. And I normally just use companies that do not charge me, residential surcharge, to deliver to residential address. For most carriers, the cost and structure is quite simple. It's basically the basic charge plus the weight charge. So altogether, you times the fuel surcharge on top and the fuel surcharge is another one. It's almost like a hidden cost when it comes to logistics because it changes week by week or month by month. It's anywhere between 15 and 20 percent. So it can be quite a large amount. So you just have to pay attention to that as well. So this cost is basically how much you're expecting to pay to deliver an item from your

## [06:05 - 06:18]

warehouse all the way to end consumer. So the actual items for this lesson is to reevaluate all the different carriers that work with and also go through the questionnaire in the workbook. Now I'll see you in the next lesson.
